title: VS Code as a Full IDE: Native Tools That Replace Your External Toolchain
content: Most full-stack developers run three to five external tools alongside VS Code — Postman for API testing, GitHub Desktop for git, a dedicated test runner in a separate terminal, and a formatter hooked into a pre-commit script. Each context switch carries a cost: broken focus, mismatched environments, and configuration that lives outside the project repository. VS Code’s native configuration layer eliminates most of that overhead — but only if you know where to look. This article covers six native tools and configurations that replace common external dependencies, reduce context switching, and keep your entire development environment version-controlled inside .vscode/. The configurations cover C#, Python, Node.js, and React, though the patterns apply broadly across any full-stack combination. 1. Code Formatting and Linting (ESLint & Prettier) The Problem Without editor-integrated formatting, styling inconsistencies accumulate silently. Developers format on different schedules — some before commits, some never — and CI linting failures become a routine part of the pull request cycle. When formatters run as a separate manual step or a pre-commit hook, they’re easy to skip, misconfigure, or forget entirely when onboarding a new machine. The Solution VS Code’s formatOnSave and codeActionsOnSave settings integrate formatting and linting directly into the save cycle. Every file is cleaned the moment it’s written, before it reaches staging, review, or CI. This removes the “I forgot to format” class of PR comments permanently. Install the ESLint and Prettier - Code Formatter extensions, then add the following to .vscode/settings.json: { "editor.formatOnSave": true, "editor.codeActionsOnSave": { "source.fixAll.eslint": "explicit" }, "editor.defaultFormatter": "esbenp.prettier-vscode", "[csharp]": { "editor.defaultFormatter": "ms-dotnettools.csharp" }, "[python]": { "editor.defaultFormatter": "ms-python.black-formatter" } } With this in place: JavaScript, TypeScript, and HTML/CSS are formatted automatically on save via Prettier. ESLint auto-fixes violations (unused variables, bad imports, rule infractions) on save. C# files are formatted according to Roslyn/OmniSharp guidelines. Python files are formatted using Black, the de facto standard in modern Python projects. Because this configuration lives in .vscode/settings.json and is committed to the repository, every developer on the team gets identical formatting behavior on clone — no separate formatter setup required. Limitations This approach is VS Code-specific. Developers using JetBrains IDEs, Neovim, or Emacs need equivalent formatter configurations in their own editors — the .vscode/ directory does nothing for them. For cross-editor teams, pairing this with a .editorconfig file and project-level ESLint/Prettier config files (eslint.config.js, .prettierrc) ensures formatting rules are enforced regardless of editor. The VS Code settings layer then becomes a convenience on top of the project-level enforcement, not a replacement for it. 2. Unified Debugging with tasks.json and launch.json The Problem A typical manual debug session in a multi-language project looks like this: open a terminal, run dotnet build, wait for compilation, switch back to the editor, open the Run and Debug panel, select the right configuration, and attach the debugger. That sequence repeats every time you make a change worth testing under the debugger — several times an hour in active development. Each step is a context switch, and any one of them can be skipped or done out of order (debugging stale binaries is a common, frustrating result). The problem compounds across languages. A full-stack developer might need to start a .NET backend, a Node.js API layer, and a React frontend in the correct order, each with its own pre-launch requirements. Doing this manually across multiple terminal tabs is error-prone and slows down every iteration cycle. The Solution VS Code’s .vscode/tasks.json and .vscode/launch.json encode the entire pre-launch sequence as a first-class IDE concern. The preLaunchTask field in a launch configuration chains tasks — format, lint, build — before the debugger attaches. Press F5, and the editor handles compilation, quality checks, and debugger attachment in the correct order automatically. Each language has a distinct pre-launch concern. The configurations below address those differences directly. C# / .NET Core C# requires compilation before debugging. The pre-launch sequence here runs dotnet format first, then dotnet build, so the binary is both up-to-date and clean before the debugger attaches. .vscode/tasks.json { "version": "2.0.0", "tasks": [ { "label": "format-csharp", "command": "dotnet", "type": "process", "args": [ "format", "${workspaceFolder}/Backend/Backend.csproj" ], "problemMatcher": [] }, { "label": "build-csharp", "command": "dotnet", "type": "process", "args": [ "build", "${workspaceFolder}/Backend/Backend.csproj", "/property:GenerateFullPaths=true", "/consoleloggerparameters:NoSummary" ], "dependsOn": [ "format-csharp" ], "problemMatcher": "$msCompile" } ] } .vscode/launch.json { "version": "0.2.0", "configurations": [ { "name": ".NET Core Launch (Backend)", "type": "coreclr", "request": "launch", "preLaunchTask": "build-csharp", "program": "${workspaceFolder}/Backend/bin/Debug/net8.0/Backend.dll", "args": [], "cwd": "${workspaceFolder}/Backend", "stopAtEntry": false, "console": "internalConsole" } ] } Node.js (Express Server) Node.js with TypeScript doesn’t compile to native binaries, but it does require a lint pass and a TypeScript transpilation step (npm run build) before the output JavaScript is safe to run under the debugger. Linting before build catches import errors and type violations that would otherwise surface as runtime exceptions mid-session. .vscode/tasks.json { "version": "2.0.0", "tasks": [ { "label": "lint-node", "type": "npm", "script": "lint", "problemMatcher": [] }, { "label": "build-node", "type": "npm", "script": "build", "dependsOn": [ "lint-node" ], "problemMatcher": [] } ] } .vscode/launch.json { "version": "0.2.0", "configurations": [ { "name": "Node.js: Express Server", "type": "node", "request": "launch", "program": "${workspaceFolder}/server/index.js", "preLaunchTask": "build-node", "skipFiles": [ "<node_internals>/**" ], "cwd": "${workspaceFolder}/server" } ] } React (Frontend) React’s development server is a long-running background process, not a one-shot build. The challenge is that the Chrome debugger cannot attach until the dev server has fully compiled and is serving on its port. The isBackground flag combined with beginsPattern and endsPattern tells VS Code to wait for the “Compiled successfully!” signal before launching Chrome — preventing the race condition where Chrome opens before the bundle is ready. .vscode/tasks.json { "version": "2.0.0", "tasks": [ { "label": "start-react-server", "type": "npm", "script": "start", "isBackground": true, "problemMatcher": { "pattern": { "regexp": "^.*$" }, "background": { "activeOnStart": true, "beginsPattern": "Starting the development server...", "endsPattern": "Compiled successfully!" } } } ] } .vscode/launch.json { "version": "0.2.0", "configurations": [ { "name": "React: Chrome Debugger", "type": "chrome", "request": "launch", "url": "http://localhost:3000", "webRoot": "${workspaceFolder}/frontend", "preLaunchTask": "start-react-server" } ] } Python Python is interpreted, so there is no compilation step. The pre-launch concern is code quality: running ruff check before the session starts catches style violations and import errors without adding meaningful latency. The launch configurations below cover both script-level debugging and Flask application debugging. .vscode/tasks.json { "version": "2.0.0", "tasks": [ { "label": "lint-python", "type": "shell", "command": "ruff check ${fileDirname}", "problemMatcher": [] } ] } .vscode/launch.json { "version": "0.2.0", "configurations": [ { "name": "Python: Current File", "type": "debugpy", "request": "launch", "program": "${file}", "console": "integratedTerminal", "justMyCode": true }, { "name": "Python: Flask App", "type": "debugpy", "request": "launch", "module": "flask", "env": { "FLASK_APP": "app.py", "FLASK_ENV": "development" }, "args": [ "run", "--no-debugger", "--no-reload" ], "jinja": true, "justMyCode": true } ] } Limitations This configuration is editor-bound. Developers not using VS Code cannot benefit from it, and in polyglot teams this can create a two-tier developer experience. Complex monorepos with nested projects can also make preLaunchTask chains brittle — a task defined in a root tasks.json may not resolve paths correctly when the debugger target is in a deeply nested subdirectory. In those cases, workspace-scoped .vscode/ directories per sub-project are necessary. Additionally, the isBackground pattern used for React is sensitive to changes in the dev server’s console output. If the endsPattern string changes across versions of Create React App or Vite, the debugger will hang waiting for a signal that never arrives. When migrating bundlers, re-validate the pattern against the new tool’s actual output. 3. Native NPM Scripts Runner The Problem Running package.json scripts from the terminal is straightforward but fragmented. In a monorepo or multi-project workspace, developers must remember which terminal tab corresponds to which project, which scripts exist, and the exact script names. New team members frequently waste time reading package.json files to discover what scripts are available — or worse, they run the wrong script in the wrong directory. The Solution VS Code’s built-in NPM Scripts Explorer surfaces all package.json scripts from every project in the workspace in a single panel. Scripts can be run, debugged, or stopped with a single click — no terminal navigation required. Enable it in .vscode/settings.json: { "npm.enableScriptExplorer": true, "npm.exclude": "**/node_modules/**" } Once enabled, an NPM SCRIPTS accordion appears at the bottom of the File Explorer sidebar. Every script from every detected package.json in the workspace is listed with clickable run and debug buttons. For keyboard-driven workflows, the Command Palette (Cmd+Shift+P / Ctrl+Shift+P) approach also works: Tasks: Run Task → npm: <script-name> Limitations The NPM Scripts Explorer is a discovery and convenience tool, not a process manager. It does not show whether a script is currently running, does not support restarting processes, and does not aggregate logs from multiple simultaneous scripts into a single view. For projects where multiple long-running scripts need to run concurrently (e.g., a backend watcher, a frontend dev server, and a test watcher), a dedicated tool like tmux or VS Code’s own compound launch configurations offers better process visibility. 4. Integrated Unit Testing via Test Explorer The Problem Running tests in a separate terminal and then returning to the editor to fix failures is a two-step feedback loop. Identifying which test failed, navigating to the correct file and line, and then re-running just that test requires manual coordination between tools. Coverage data typically lives in a separate HTML report that must be opened in a browser. Debugging a failing test — stepping through it with a debugger — usually requires writing a separate debug script or using a test runner flag that most developers don’t bother with. The Solution VS Code’s native Test Explorer (the beaker icon in the sidebar) provides a unified interface for discovering, running, debugging, and inspecting test results directly inside the editor. Install the appropriate language-specific extension, add minimal configuration, and test results appear inline next to the code they cover. Language / Framework Extension Configuration C# (.NET) C# Dev Kit / .NET Test Explorer Native detection of xUnit, NUnit, MSTest Node.js / React Jest / Vitest (by Vitest) "vitest.enable": true Python Python (Microsoft) Pytest or Unittest via settings The critical capability that separates this from running tests in a terminal is in-editor debugging: place a breakpoint inside a test function, click the debug button next to that test in the Explorer, and the debugger attaches for that single test case. This makes diagnosing assertion failures and unexpected exceptions significantly faster than print-debugging or log analysis. Python Pytest (.vscode/settings.json) { "python.testing.pytestArgs": [ "tests" ], "python.testing.unittestEnabled": false, "python.testing.pytestEnabled": true } Node.js / React Jest (.vscode/settings.json) { "jest.disabledWorkspaceFolders": [], "jest.enableCodeLens": true, "jest.shell": "/bin/zsh" } Limitations Test Explorer extensions vary significantly in stability and feature coverage. The Jest extension for VS Code has historically had configuration sensitivity issues — particularly with projects using custom Jest configurations, moduleNameMapper overrides, or non-standard project structures. Vitest’s extension is newer and more reliable for modern setups but lacks some of the Jest extension’s code lens features. For C#, the .NET Test Explorer requires the C# Dev Kit, which is a Microsoft-licensed extension and may not be available in all VS Code forks (e.g., VSCodium). Coverage highlighting inside the editor also depends on the extension generating coverage data in a format it understands — not all test runner configurations produce this automatically. 5. Built-in Git & Source Control Integration The Problem Git CLI is powerful and portable, but it introduces friction during high-velocity development cycles. Resolving a three-way merge conflict in the terminal requires running git diff --cc to view both sides of the conflict, editing the file manually to remove conflict markers, and then staging and committing. Stashing work in progress requires remembering the right combination of git stash push -u -m "description". For developers primarily focused on writing code rather than mastering git internals, these friction points accumulate. The Solution VS Code’s native Source Control panel provides a graphical interface for the full git lifecycle without requiring a third-party tool. Staging, committing, pushing, pulling, stashing, branching, and conflict resolution are all accessible from a single sidebar panel. VS Code native Source Control panel showing changes, staging, stashing, and push menus. The most significant productivity gain is in conflict resolution. When a merge produces conflicts, VS Code opens a dedicated Merge Editor — a three-pane view showing the current branch, the incoming branch, and a merged result pane. Accepting changes from either side, or combining both, is a single click. This is materially faster and less error-prone than manually editing conflict markers in a terminal. Key workflows in the Source Control panel: Cloning: Cmd+Shift+P → Git: Clone → paste repository URL. VS Code clones and prompts to open the folder immediately. Staging & Committing: Click + next to files in the Changes list to stage. Type the commit message in the input box and press Cmd+Enter / Ctrl+Enter. Pushing & Pulling: Sync Changes pushes local commits and fetches upstream changes in one operation. Stashing: ... menu → Stash → Stash (Include Untracked). Restore via ... → Stash → Pop Stash. Merging & Conflict Resolution: Git: Merge Branch... from the Command Palette. Conflicts open automatically in the Merge Editor. Limitations The graphical Source Control panel is designed for linear, day-to-day git workflows. Complex operations — interactive rebase, cherry-picking across multiple commits, bisecting, or reflog recovery — remain significantly easier in the CLI or in a dedicated git GUI like GitKraken or Sourcetree. Attempting these operations through the Command Palette in VS Code often requires multiple steps where a single CLI command would suffice. The Source Control panel also does not give a visual overview of branch topology or commit graph. For repositories with active parallel branches or long release histories, a tool like GitLens (VS Code extension) or a standalone git GUI provides substantially better visibility into branch relationships. 6. REST Client with Multi-Environment & Azure AD Config The Problem Postman and similar GUI tools solve API testing, but they introduce a critical separation: the API test collection lives outside the codebase. When the API changes, the tests must be updated in Postman separately — and that update may or may not happen. Collections stored in Postman’s cloud are also subject to their team plan pricing and are not automatically synchronized with the repository. For teams practicing infrastructure-as-code or treating their .http files as executable documentation, this separation is a liability. The Solution The REST Client extension (by Huachao Mao) allows HTTP requests to be written in plain .http files and executed directly inside VS Code. These files live in the repository alongside the code they test, are version-controlled, and can reference environment variables defined in .vscode/settings.json. The result is an API test suite that is always in sync with the backend it targets. Step 1: Define environments in .vscode/settings.json { "rest-client.environmentVariables": { "$shared": { "tenantId": "your-azure-ad-tenant-id", "clientId": "your-azure-ad-client-app-id" }, "local": { "baseUrl": "http://localhost:5000", "audience": "api://localhost-backend" }, "staging": { "baseUrl": "https://api-staging.example.com", "audience": "https://api.example.com" }, "production": { "baseUrl": "https://api.example.com", "audience": "https://api.example.com" } } } Step 2: Write .http files with Azure AD authentication The REST Client supports named requests, allowing the token response from one request to be captured and reused as a variable in subsequent requests. This makes OAuth2 client credentials flows — common in Azure AD / Entra ID protected APIs — straightforward to automate: # @name getAADToken POST https://login.microsoftonline.com//oauth2/v2.0/token HTTP/1.1 Content-Type: application/x-www-form-urlencoded grant_type=client_credentials &client_id= &client_secret= &scope=/.default ### Cache the Token Variable @authToken = ### Secured API Request GET /api/v1/users HTTP/1.1 Authorization: Bearer Content-Type: application/json Switch environments using the environment selector in the VS Code status bar. All `` references update automatically. Recommended file organization: Structure .http files to mirror the API’s controller layout — one file per controller or resource, grouped under a shared directory (e.g., api-tests/users.http, api-tests/orders.http). Within a file, separate individual requests with ###, which the REST Client treats as a request delimiter. This allows multiple variations of the same endpoint (e.g., valid request, missing auth header, malformed payload) to coexist in a single file without ambiguity. api-tests/ auth.http users.http orders.http products.http This structure scales cleanly as the API grows and makes it immediately clear which .http file covers which part of the backend. For the full feature set — request history, cookies, GraphQL support, and response variable chaining — see the REST Client extension documentation. Limitations The REST Client is a capable replacement for Postman in solo and small-team contexts, but it lacks several features that larger teams rely on. There is no shared cloud workspace — collaboration requires committing .http files to the repository and ensuring team members have the extension installed. There are no mock server capabilities, no automated test runner that produces structured pass/fail reports, and no GUI for browsing response history across sessions. For teams that need Postman’s collaboration features, collection runner, or Newman CI integration, the REST Client is a complement rather than a full replacement. Note on secrets: The clientSecret variable in the .http file above should never be committed to the repository. Store it as a user-level environment variable or reference it from a .env file that is included in .gitignore. Summary: A Version-Controlled Development Environment The six configurations above share a common principle: development environment setup belongs in the repository, not in each developer’s head. By committing .vscode/tasks.json, .vscode/launch.json, .vscode/settings.json, and .http files alongside the application code, the workspace becomes self-documenting and immediately reproducible. The practical outcomes: Reduced onboarding time: A developer who clones the repository gets a fully configured formatter, debugger, test runner, and API client on first open — no separate setup documentation required. Eliminated context switching: Building, debugging, testing, and API validation happen inside a single editor window. External tools are no longer a requirement for the core development loop. Consistent code quality: Formatting and linting happen on every save across every developer’s machine, removing a class of review comments and CI failures. Version-controlled API tests: .http files evolve with the API they test, in the same commit, reviewed in the same pull request. These configurations work best as a team-wide commitment. A .vscode/ directory that one developer maintains and others ignore provides none of the onboarding or consistency benefits. The highest-leverage first step is committing a baseline settings.json with formatOnSave enabled — it is the lowest-friction change with the broadest impact across every language in the stack.

title: Engineering at Scale: Notes on Architecture, Cloud, and AI
content: Welcome. This is where I document the engineering decisions, architectural patterns, and research that shape the systems I build. The writing here is practical and opinionated — drawn from real-world delivery, not theory alone. About Me I am a Full-Stack Software Architect focused on designing and delivering scalable, resilient, cloud-native systems across AWS, GCP, and Azure. My work spans the full lifecycle — from architecture and design through implementation, deployment, and operation at scale. With an M.Tech in Data Science, I work at the intersection of enterprise software architecture and modern data systems. My current focus and ongoing research center on: Artificial Intelligence & Machine Learning (AI/ML) Generative AI (GenAI) & Large Language Models (LLMs) Scalable Distributed Systems & Multi-Cloud Architecture What to Expect I publish technical articles, architectural guides, and research notes grounded in production experience: System Design & Cloud Architecture: Proven patterns and trade-off analysis for building multi-cloud solutions that scale. AI & Data Engineering: In-depth treatments of machine learning workflows, data pipeline engineering, and applied GenAI. Modern Web & Full-Stack Development: Practical guidance and best practices for building robust, maintainable applications. Expect rigor over hype and depth over breadth. The first articles are on the way.
